Public Investment Risk Analysis in the Dominican Republic

Gari Jerez

2026-03-28

1. Introduction

This project analyzes public investment projects using data from the Ministry of Economy, Planning and Development (MEPyD).
The objective is to evaluate project performance using financial indicators such as Net Present Value (NPV), sector investment trends, and risk simulation using Monte Carlo methods.

2. Libraries

3. Load Data

4. Data Cleaning

5. Net Present Value Calculation

The Net Present Value (NPV) of each project is calculated using a discount rate of 12%.
The indicator measures the economic profitability of each investment project by discounting future execution flows to their present value.

10. Monte Carlo Simulation

Monte Carlo simulation is used to evaluate project risk.

Figure 10. Monte Carlo Simulation of Net Present Value for the Selected Project

11. Cumulative Distribution Function

Figure 11. Cumulative Distribution Function of Simulated Net Present Value

12. Conclusion

The analysis reveals substantial heterogeneity in the performance of public investment projects across sectors and regions. The Net Present Value (NPV) results indicate that while some sectors generate consistently positive returns, others exhibit lower profitability and higher dispersion.

The distribution and relationship analysis suggest that project duration does not guarantee higher returns, highlighting the importance of efficient resource allocation and project management.

From a risk perspective, the Monte Carlo simulation provides critical insights into the uncertainty surrounding project outcomes. The results show not only the expected range of returns but also the probability of negative NPV, offering a more comprehensive framework for decision-making under uncertainty.

Overall, this analysis demonstrates the value of combining financial evaluation techniques with statistical and geospatial methods to support evidence-based public investment decisions.

Future work could extend this approach by incorporating macroeconomic variables, sector-specific risk factors, and machine learning models to improve predictive accuracy.
